by Andy Demetra (The Voice of the Yellow Jackets) Inside The Chart On The Map: The upbringing that makes newly minted special teams coordinator Jason Semore unlike almost any coach in Division I football You’ve read this story before. Kid grows up a small-town coach’s son. Follows his dad into the business.  Grinds his way up the coaching ladder, establishing his own success. That doesn’t make Jason Semore all that different from the scores of other coaches’ sons who populate the staffs around college football. After playing for his dad in high school, then lettering in college, Semore has spent the past 17 years hopscotching from Division II to FCS to FBS, from one side of the country to the other, from small-school coordinator to Power-5 analyst and now, finally, to linebackers coach at Georgia Tech. On Monday he was promoted to the Yellow Jackets’ special teams coordinator, a role he also held in 2015 at the University of Montana. But Semore’s background makes him unlike any coach’s son at the Division I level. Maybe any coach in Division I, period. And the answer lies innocuously at the top of his bio. Hometown: Ganado, Ariz. It’s an easy piece of information to gloss over, or skim past, or remain incurious about. Every coach and player has it listed obligatorily on their roster page.

So, let’s talk about Semore’s coaching journey for a bit. He kicked things off back in 2006 at Round Valley High School in Arizona as the Defensive Coordinator. Then, in 2007, he moved on to the Colorado School of Mines as a Secondary Coach. From 2008 to 2009, he was at Adams State, where he juggled roles as the Co-Defensive Coordinator, Secondary Coach, and Special Teams Coordinator. After that, he spent 2010 and 2011 coaching Outside Linebackers at Tulsa. 

Then he joined Oklahoma State as a Defensive Assistant from 2012 to 2013 before heading back to Colorado School of Mines as the Defensive Coordinator in 2014. In 2015, he took on the role of Secondary Coach and Special Teams Coordinator at Montana, and then served as the Defensive Coordinator there from 2016 to 2017. 

In 2018, he was with Temple as a Senior Defensive Advisor, followed by a stint at Georgia Tech as a Defensive Analyst from 2019 to 2020. Most recently, in 2021, he stepped in as the Offensive Coordinator at Valdosta State. 

Honestly, he’s got way more experience than I remembered, but this is definitely the highest position he’s held so far, which is bound to come with its own set of challenges. At Valdosta State, his offense was pretty impressive, ranking in the top 20 nationwide for offensive touchdowns, pass efficiency, red zone offense, and turnover ratios. They even made it to the national championship game, though they didn’t quite snag the trophy. 

Now, when we look at the coaches he’s working alongside, they’re coming from pretty different backgrounds, but I feel like they’re all stepping in to replace Andrew Thacker in a way. He’s still the defensive coordinator in title, but he’s no longer the linebackers coach.

But it was really at West Virginia that he hit his stride, leading the Big 12 in completion percentage back in 2014 and even becoming a semifinalist for the Davey O’Brien Award. Fast forward to 2023, and we’ve got Jason Semore joining the Marshall coaching staff as the offensive coordinator for the Thundering Herd. He’s coming off a season with Georgia Tech, adding to his already impressive coaching resume that covers a bunch of roles at different levels. Semore played linebacker at Adams State from 2001 to 2005, and in 2022, he was busy coaching the Georgia Tech linebackers, with plans to step up as their special teams coordinator focusing on nickel backs and safeties. But instead, he’s back in a familiar role that he’s held before at Valdosta State, Montana, and Colorado School of Mines, plus some co-OC duties at his old school. And let me tell you, he’s had a lot of success wherever he’s been! He helped the Colorado School of Mines to a 10-2 season, was part of Montana’s three straight winning seasons with a top-ranked Big Sky defense, and his defensive squad at Valdosta State made a run to the DII title game in 2021.
